stem: Apply the Gram–Schmidt process to \( \mathbf{u}_1 = ([[a1]], [[a2]], [[a3]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[b1]], [[b2]], [[b3]]) \). The second orthogonal vector is \( \mathbf{w}_2 = \mathbf{v}_2 - \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_2\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}{\mathbf{u}_1\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}\,\mathbf{u}_1 \). What is the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{w}_2 \)?
solution: Subtract the projection of \( \mathbf{v}_2 \) onto \( \mathbf{u}_1 \): the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{w}_2 \) is \( ([[b1]]) - \dfrac{([[dot]])}{[[uu]]}\cdot([[a1]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the projection instead of subtracting it.
This omits the factor \( u_1 \) (the first component of \( \mathbf{u}_1 \)) in the projection term.
This gives the projection's first coordinate rather than the residual \( \mathbf{w}_2 \).
This returns \( \mathbf{v}_2 \) unchanged, as if no projection were subtracted.
This negates the original coordinate instead of removing the projection.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: Apply Gram–Schmidt to \( \mathbf{u}_1 = ([[a1]], [[a2]], [[a3]]) \), \( \mathbf{u}_2 = ([[a2]], [[negA1]], 0) \) (already orthogonal to \( \mathbf{u}_1 \)), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[v3x]], [[v3y]], [[v3z]]) \). The third orthogonal vector is \( \mathbf{w}_3 = \mathbf{v}_3 - \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_3\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}{\mathbf{u}_1\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}\mathbf{u}_1 - \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_3\cdot\mathbf{u}_2}{\mathbf{u}_2\cdot\mathbf{u}_2}\mathbf{u}_2 \). What is the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{w}_3 \)?
solution: Subtract projections onto BOTH earlier orthogonal vectors: \( w_{3,1} = [[v3x]] - \dfrac{[[dot13]]}{[[w1w1]]}\cdot[[a1]] - \dfrac{[[dot23]]}{[[w2w2]]}\cdot[[a2]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds both projections instead of subtracting them.
This removes only the projection onto \( \mathbf{u}_1 \), forgetting \( \mathbf{u}_2 \).
This removes only the projection onto \( \mathbf{u}_2 \), forgetting \( \mathbf{u}_1 \).
This returns \( \mathbf{v}_3 \) unchanged, as if no projection were subtracted.
This negates the original coordinate instead of removing the projections.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.

stem: The vector \( \mathbf{w} = ([[wx]], [[wy]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^2 \) is rescaled to the unit vector \( \hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w}/\|\mathbf{w}\| \) (the first step of building an orthonormal basis). What is the first coordinate of \( \hat{\mathbf{w}} \)?
solution: \( \|\mathbf{w}\| = \sqrt{([[wx]])^2+([[wy]])^2} = [[r]] \), so the first coordinate of \( \hat{\mathbf{w}} \) is \( [[wx]]/[[r]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the SECOND coordinate of \( \hat{\mathbf{w}} \), not the first.
This divides by \( \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 \) instead of \( \|\mathbf{w}\| \) (forgot the square root).
This multiplies by the norm instead of dividing by it.
This has the wrong sign.
This is the original (un-normalized) coordinate, as if \( \|\mathbf{w}\|=1 \) already.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.

stem: For what value of \( t \) are the vectors \( \mathbf{u} = ([[a]], t, [[b]]) \) and \( \mathbf{w} = ([[c]], [[d]], [[e]]) \) orthogonal (a requirement for them to appear together in an orthonormal basis)?
distractor_rationales: This drops the minus sign that comes from isolating \( t \).
This subtracts instead of adds the two known-coordinate products before dividing.
This forgets to divide by the coefficient \( d \) of \( t \).
This just sums all five given numbers, ignoring the orthogonality equation.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: The columns of a \( 2\times 2 \) orthogonal matrix \( Q \) are orthonormal. Its first column is \( \left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[r]]},\ \dfrac{[[q]]}{[[r]]}\right) \), its second column is a unit vector perpendicular to the first, and \( \det(Q) = [[orient]] \). What is the second entry (the \(y\)-coordinate) of the second column of \( Q \)?
solution: A unit vector perpendicular to \( (p/r, q/r) \) is \( \pm(-q/r,\ p/r) \); the sign making \( \det(Q) = [[orient]] \) gives second column \( \left(\dfrac{-([[orient]])\cdot[[q]]}{[[r]]},\ \dfrac{([[orient]])\cdot[[p]]}{[[r]]}\right) \), so its \(y\)-coordinate is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the FIRST entry of the second column (its \(x\)-coordinate), not the second.
This copies the first column's corresponding entry instead of the second column's.
This has the wrong sign for the given determinant.
This swaps which coordinate (\(p\) vs \(q\)) goes with the second entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This doubles the correct entry.
